news_summary: Today's news highlights a strong call for enhanced support and justice for victims alongside a celebration of unity, values, and democracy in Europe.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en avant un appel pressant en faveur d'un soutien accru et d'une meilleure justice pour les victimes, tout en célébrant l'unité, les valeurs et la démocratie en Europe.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy ponen de relieve un firme llamamiento a reforzar el apoyo y la justicia para las víctimas, al tiempo que se celebra la unidad, los valores y la democracia en Europa.

articles.title: ‘Victims need a voice, assistance and justice’: UN victims’ rights advocate calls for stronger action
articles.summary: The United Nations Victims’ Rights Advocate has called for stronger and sustained action on behalf of the people she serves across the UN system.
articles.content:
Najla Nassif Palma told UN News in an interview this week that Member States need to better support survivors of sexual exploitation and abuse by UN personnel, warning that much more remains to be done to ensure victims receive justice, protection and long-term assistance.

Speaking after the release of her 2024–2025 annual report, Ms. Nassif Palma said her mandate is centred on ensuring that victims are “not invisible” and that they are guaranteed “a voice, assistance and justice”.

Established by Secretary-General António Guterres in 2017 as part of the UN’s strategy to strengthen protection from sexual exploitation and abuse, the Office of the Victims’ Rights Advocate works across the UN system to advance a victim-centred approach and ensure survivors’ perspectives shape prevention, response and accountability efforts.

Ms. Nassif Palma, only the second senior official to hold the post, said direct engagement with victims has been one of the most important aspects of her work.

Over the past two years, she has visited more than 10 countries and met survivors directly to better understand their experiences, concerns and priorities.

“The first step is to listen to the victims,” she highlighted, describing their courage and resilience as a driving force behind her advocacy.

Her office works closely with Senior Victims’ Rights Officers and Victims’ Rights Focal Points deployed in the field, who support survivors in their own communities by helping connect them with medical care, psychosocial support, legal assistance, education and livelihood opportunities.

UN Photo/Nektarios Markogiannis

Expanding assistance and support

The report highlights progress in expanding assistance for victims and children born of exploitation and abuse, including access to school fee support, skills training and income-generating activities designed to help survivors rebuild their independence and dignity.

According to Ms. Nassif Palma, more UN entities — including peacekeeping missions such as MINUSCA in the Central African Republic, MONUSCO in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and UNMISS in South Sudan — are now allocating dedicated resources to victim assistance.

“We are improving how the UN listens to victims and includes their voices in decisions that affect them,” she said.

She stressed the importance of partnerships with civil society organisations, particularly women-led groups working directly with victims at community level, to ensure survivors’ experiences inform UN policies and programmes.

The report also outlines efforts to strengthen victims’ rights throughout UN investigative, disciplinary and judicial processes.

Ms. Nassif Palma said survivors must be fully informed about the status of their cases, protected from retaliation and free to decide whether they wish to participate in investigations.

Soundcloud

Paternity and child support claims

Another priority has been working with governments to advance paternity and child support claims.

Some Member States have already taken steps to provide lump-sum payments or interim financial support to victims and children while legal proceedings continue, according to the report.

Despite the progress, Ms. Nassif Palma warned that significant challenges remain.

She called for more specialised personnel dedicated to supporting victims on the ground, more sustainable and predictable funding for assistance programmes, and stronger political commitment from both the UN system and Member States.

“We need more support for victims at every step of the accountability process, including protection, clear updates on their cases, and real progress on long-standing paternity and child support claims,” she said.

‘My voice is here’ for you

Her message to survivors was clear: “Come forward. We are here to support you. My voice is here to advocate for your rights”

She also urged governments and UN entities to translate commitments into concrete action.

“To move forward, we need sustained commitment across the UN system and Member States to truly put victims’ rights first and turn promises into real change in their lives,” she said.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the UN Victims’ Rights Advocate's ongoing efforts to improve justice, protection, and assistance for survivors of sexual exploitation and abuse by UN personnel. It highlights concrete progress in victim support programs, expanded resources in peacekeeping missions, and advocacy for victims' voices to be heard, which has broad societal significance and a positive real-world impact on vulnerable populations globally.
articles.personality_title: UN advocate pushes for stronger support for survivors of abuse by UN personnel
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The United Nations has a special advocate who works to help people affected by sexual exploitation and abuse by UN staff. This role was created in 2017 to make sure victims get justice and support.

**What happened** – Najla Nassif Palma, the current UN Victims’ Rights Advocate, shared her 2024–2025 report and called for stronger actions from countries and the UN. She has visited over 10 countries to listen to survivors directly and understand their needs better.

**Impact** – The advocate’s work has led to more help for survivors, including medical care, education, and job training. UN peacekeeping missions in countries like the Central African Republic and South Sudan are now giving more resources to support victims. Survivors are also getting more information about their cases and better protection.

**What’s next step** – Ms. Nassif Palma wants more trained staff to support victims and steady funding to keep these programs going. She also urges governments to take real action on issues like child support for children born from abuse. The goal is to make sure victims’ voices guide UN decisions and that promises turn into real help.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN Victims’ Rights Advocate is working to ensure survivors of abuse by UN personnel receive justice, support, and a stronger voice in decisions affecting their lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: La défenseure des droits des victimes de l’ONU appelle à un soutien renforcé pour les survivants d’abus
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Organisation des Nations Unies a une défenseure spéciale qui aide les personnes victimes d’exploitation sexuelle et d’abus commis par son personnel. Ce poste a été créé en 2017 pour garantir justice et soutien aux victimes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Najla Nassif Palma, la défenseure actuelle des droits des victimes, a présenté son rapport 2024–2025 et a demandé des actions plus fortes des pays et de l’ONU. Elle a visité plus de 10 pays pour écouter directement les survivants et mieux comprendre leurs besoins.

**Impact** – Son travail a permis d’offrir plus d’aide aux survivants, comme des soins médicaux, l’éducation et la formation professionnelle. Les missions de maintien de la paix de l’ONU dans des pays comme la République centrafricaine et le Soudan du Sud allouent désormais plus de ressources pour soutenir les victimes. Les survivants reçoivent aussi plus d’informations sur leurs dossiers et une meilleure protection.

**Prochaine étape** – Mme Nassif Palma souhaite plus de personnel formé pour soutenir les victimes et un financement stable pour ces programmes. Elle encourage aussi les gouvernements à agir concrètement sur des sujets comme le soutien aux enfants nés d’abus. L’objectif est de faire en sorte que la voix des victimes guide les décisions de l’ONU et que les promesses deviennent de l’aide réelle.

**En une phrase** – La défenseure des droits des victimes de l’ONU œuvre pour que les survivants d’abus par le personnel de l’ONU obtiennent justice, soutien et une voix plus forte dans les décisions qui les concernent.
articles.personality_title_es: La defensora de derechos de las víctimas de la ONU pide más apoyo para sobrevivientes de abusos por personal de la ONU
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Organización de las Naciones Unidas tiene una defensora especial que ayuda a personas afectadas por explotación sexual y abusos cometidos por su personal. Este puesto se creó en 2017 para garantizar justicia y apoyo a las víctimas.

**Qué pasó** – Najla Nassif Palma, la actual defensora de derechos de las víctimas, presentó su informe 2024–2025 y pidió acciones más fuertes por parte de los países y la ONU. Ha visitado más de 10 países para escuchar directamente a los sobrevivientes y entender mejor sus necesidades.

**Impacto** – Su trabajo ha logrado brindar más ayuda a los sobrevivientes, incluyendo atención médica, educación y capacitación laboral. Las misiones de paz de la ONU en países como la República Centroafricana y Sudán del Sur ahora destinan más recursos para apoyar a las víctimas. Los sobrevivientes también reciben más información sobre sus casos y mejor protección.

**Próximo paso** – La Sra. Nassif Palma quiere más personal capacitado para apoyar a las víctimas y financiamiento estable para mantener estos programas. También insta a los gobiernos a tomar medidas concretas sobre temas como el apoyo a los hijos nacidos de abusos. El objetivo es que la voz de las víctimas guíe las decisiones de la ONU y que las promesas se conviertan en ayuda real.

**Una frase clave** – La defensora de derechos de las víctimas de la ONU trabaja para que los sobrevivientes de abusos por parte del personal de la ONU reciban justicia, apoyo y una voz más fuerte en las decisiones que afectan sus vidas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a circle of diverse, gently glowing lanterns held aloft by silhouetted hands rising from a peaceful gathering of abstract figures, symbolizing the collective voice, support, and resilience of survivors, set against a soft, natural color palette with subtle threads of light connecting the lanterns to represent justice, assistance, and community empowerment.

articles.title: Press release - Europeans celebrate unity, values and democracy on Europe Day 2026
articles.summary: On 9 May, Europeans will celebrate Europe Day. Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
This year marks the 76th anniversary of the Schuman Declaration, which laid the foundations for the European Union as we know it today, and led to an unprecedented era of peace, democracy, prosperity, integration and cooperation across the continent.

In 2026, Europe Day also marks two important milestones: 40 years since Portugal and Spain joined the EU, and 40 years since the first official Europe Day celebrations.

To mark the occasion, many events will take place across EU Member States and beyond, bringing together citizens from all walks of life. The EU institutions will open their doors to visitors, offering educational activities about their work, as happens every year.

Landmark buildings and monuments across Europe and around the world will be illuminated in the EU colours.

In a rapidly changing world, the EU and its institutions are working to protect democracy, boost prosperity and strengthen security. Europe Day is an opportunity for citizens and their institutions to come together to celebrate their shared community and achievements.

The European Parliament invites citizens to discover how EU legislation shapes everyday life and how they can influence Europe's future, under the motto ‘Come and See Democracy in Action.’ Doors open at 10:00 CEST in Brussels with a performance by the European Parliament Choir, followed by a solemn opening ceremony at 11:00 CEST in the Hemicycle, with addresses by European Parliament President Roberta Metsola and Vice-President Sophie Wilmès. Visitors can take part in quizzes, presentations and discussions, enjoy family-friendly activities, and follow a live music stage on the Esplanade. In Luxembourg, activities will include guided tours of the Europa Experience and an exhibition on the fight against disinformation. The following day, a rich cultural programme is planned in Wiltz, in the presence of H.R.H. the Grand Duke of Luxembourg.

The European Council / Council of the European Union will open its doors for guided tours in the Justus Lipsius building in Brussels, offering a rare look at where key European decisions are made. Each of the 27 Member States will host a stand showcasing their culture, traditions and culinary specialties. Children will enjoy a treasure hunt and a fun fact quest, while a photo booth will allow visitors to take a selfie on the red carpet, in the shoes of an EU leader.

The European Commission will open its iconic Berlaymont building in Brussels to the public starting at 10:00 CEST with an address by Executive Vice-President Teresa Ribera. Under the slogan ‘Europe's Moment’, visitors will explore interactive thematic spaces covering democracy and values, climate, prosperity, social justice, security and Europe's global role, alongside an art and architecture trail. The celebrations will continue into the evening with free live music from 18:30 CEST at Place des Palais in Brussels.

The European Central Bank will take part in Frankfurt's Europa-Fest on the historic Römerberg market square, alongside the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority and the Anti-Money Laundering Authority, where visitors will meet experts and enjoy interactive games. The European Central Bank will also be present at the Europa Building in Brussels, where experts will answer questions about the euro, the new banknote redesign and the digital euro project.

The European External Action Service will open its Brussels headquarters from 10:00 CEST to 18:00 CEST, inviting visitors to ‘Step inside EU Diplomacy.’ The programme includes interactive exhibits, meetings with EU ambassadors, and live video calls with EU staff worldwide. The event will also feature ‘The World in One Day’, a cultural celebration with music, dance, freestyle football, crafts and family activities. An opening ceremony will take place at 11:00 CEST in the presence of representatives of partner countries and international organisations.

The European Committee of the Regions will open its doors in Brussels on Europe Day, inviting visitors to discover how the institution representing Europe's regions and cities works, and what regional and local elected politicians do for them. Visitors will learn about the Committee's role and activities, explore its political groups, and experience European cultural diversity at the Festival of Regions and Cities, which will showcase projects, arts and crafts, and offer tastings of local produce.

The European Economic and Social Committee will welcome the public to its Brussels premises at rue Belliard 99-101 from 10:00 CEST to 18:00 CEST. Visitors will meet members and staff, join guided tours in different EU languages, and discover how civil society shapes EU policies. The programme includes thematic stands, a puzzle sticker game, a caricaturist, a postcard station, live music and children's activities. French EESC members will also take part in the ‘Fête d'Europe’ in Paris.

The European Investment Bank Group Permanent Representation will showcase the Group’s role and activities in the Europa building in Brussels. Interactive quizzes and videos will highlight investments across the 27 EU Member States. On 10 May, colleagues from headquarters in Luxembourg will host the same stand at the EU Village in Wiltz, Luxembourg.

In Strasbourg and Luxembourg, EU institutions will also open their doors in May. The European Parliament's Strasbourg Open Day will take place on 17 May, with visits to the hemicycle and a full civic and cultural programme. Luxembourg will mark Europe Day on 9 May with cultural events and information stands at the Parliament's Grand Duchy premises.

Across the EU and around the world, in every Member State, European Commission Representations and European Parliament Liaison Offices will organise local Europe Day events — from public debates and school visits to exhibitions and cultural gatherings. Beyond Europe, EU delegations will mark the occasion with public events and outreach worldwide. Landmark buildings across the globe will be illuminated in EU colours.

Background

Today, the European Commission published a new Eurobarometer survey, which shows that a great majority of Europeans see benefits from EU membership and see the EU as a pillar of stability and security. Nearly three quarters of Europeans believe their country has benefited from being a member of the EU. In a challenging global context, Europeans increasingly see the EU as a source of stability, alongside record-high backing for a common defence and security policy. Three quarters of respondents (75%) say they feel they are citizens of the EU, matching the highest level ever recorded in spring 2025.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the celebration of Europe Day, marking significant milestones in European unity, democracy, peace, and cooperation. It emphasizes broad public engagement through cultural, educational, and democratic activities across all EU member states and beyond, promoting democratic values and citizen participation. The story has a meaningful positive impact on society by fostering unity, democracy, and shared values on a large scale with detailed context and planned events.
articles.personality_title: Europe Day 2026 celebrates 76 years of unity with events across the continent
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**  
Europe Day is celebrated every year on May 9 to remember the Schuman Declaration from 1949. This declaration started the idea of countries in Europe working together to keep peace and build a better future. In 2026, Europe Day also marks 40 years since Portugal and Spain joined the European Union (EU) and 40 years since the first official Europe Day celebrations.

**What happened**  
On May 9, 2026, many events took place across the 27 EU countries and beyond. EU institutions like the European Parliament, European Commission, and others opened their doors to the public. Visitors enjoyed tours, quizzes, music, and family activities. Famous buildings were lit up in EU colors. In Brussels, people learned about how the EU works and how they can take part in democracy. In other cities like Luxembourg, Frankfurt, and Strasbourg, cultural programs and exhibitions were held. Many countries showed their traditions and food, and children had special games and treasure hunts.

**Impact**  
This celebration shows how the EU brings people together from different countries to share values like peace, democracy, and cooperation. It helps citizens understand how the EU affects their daily lives and encourages them to be active in shaping Europe’s future. The events also highlight the EU’s role in protecting democracy and working on important issues like climate and security.

**What’s next step**  
Following Europe Day, the EU institutions will continue their educational and cultural programs. The European Parliament will hold an Open Day in Strasbourg on May 17. Local EU offices across member countries and worldwide will keep organizing events to connect with citizens. The EU will also keep focusing on policies that promote unity, security, and prosperity.

**One-sentence takeaway**  
Europe Day 2026 brings people together across Europe to celebrate decades of cooperation, democracy, and shared values through engaging events and activities.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Journée de l’Europe 2026 célèbre 76 ans d’unité avec des événements à travers le continent
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**  
La Journée de l’Europe est célébrée chaque année le 9 mai pour rappeler la Déclaration Schuman de 1949. Cette déclaration a lancé l’idée que les pays européens travaillent ensemble pour maintenir la paix et construire un avenir meilleur. En 2026, cette journée marque aussi 40 ans depuis l’adhésion du Portugal et de l’Espagne à l’Union européenne (UE) et 40 ans depuis les premières célébrations officielles.

**Ce qui s’est passé**  
Le 9 mai 2026, de nombreux événements ont eu lieu dans les 27 pays de l’UE et au-delà. Les institutions européennes comme le Parlement européen, la Commission européenne et d’autres ont ouvert leurs portes au public. Les visiteurs ont participé à des visites, quiz, concerts et activités familiales. Des bâtiments célèbres ont été illuminés aux couleurs de l’UE. À Bruxelles, les gens ont découvert le fonctionnement de l’UE et comment participer à la démocratie. Dans d’autres villes comme Luxembourg, Francfort et Strasbourg, des programmes culturels et des expositions ont été organisés. Plusieurs pays ont présenté leurs traditions et spécialités culinaires, et les enfants ont profité de jeux et chasses au trésor.

**Impact**  
Cette célébration montre comment l’UE rassemble des personnes de différents pays pour partager des valeurs comme la paix, la démocratie et la coopération. Elle aide les citoyens à comprendre l’impact de l’UE sur leur vie quotidienne et les encourage à s’impliquer dans l’avenir de l’Europe. Les événements mettent aussi en lumière le rôle de l’UE dans la protection de la démocratie et la gestion de questions importantes comme le climat et la sécurité.

**Prochaine étape**  
Après la Journée de l’Europe, les institutions européennes poursuivront leurs programmes éducatifs et culturels. Le Parlement européen organisera une Journée portes ouvertes à Strasbourg le 17 mai. Les bureaux locaux de l’UE dans les pays membres et dans le monde continueront d’organiser des événements pour rapprocher les citoyens. L’UE continuera aussi à se concentrer sur des politiques favorisant l’unité, la sécurité et la prospérité.

**Message clé en une phrase**  
La Journée de l’Europe 2026 rassemble les peuples à travers l’Europe pour célébrer des décennies de coopération, de démocratie et de valeurs partagées grâce à des événements et activités engageants.
articles.personality_title_es: El Día de Europa 2026 celebra 76 años de unidad con eventos en todo el continente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**  
El Día de Europa se celebra cada año el 9 de mayo para recordar la Declaración Schuman de 1949. Esta declaración inició la idea de que los países europeos trabajaran juntos para mantener la paz y construir un futuro mejor. En 2026, el Día de Europa también marca 40 años desde que Portugal y España se unieron a la Unión Europea (UE) y 40 años desde las primeras celebraciones oficiales.

**Qué pasó**  
El 9 de mayo de 2026, se realizaron muchos eventos en los 27 países de la UE y más allá. Las instituciones de la UE como el Parlamento Europeo, la Comisión Europea y otras abrieron sus puertas al público. Los visitantes disfrutaron de recorridos, concursos, música y actividades para familias. Edificios famosos se iluminaron con los colores de la UE. En Bruselas, las personas aprendieron cómo funciona la UE y cómo pueden participar en la democracia. En otras ciudades como Luxemburgo, Frankfurt y Estrasburgo, se realizaron programas culturales y exposiciones. Muchos países mostraron sus tradiciones y comidas, y los niños tuvieron juegos especiales y búsquedas del tesoro.

**Impacto**  
Esta celebración muestra cómo la UE une a personas de diferentes países para compartir valores como la paz, la democracia y la cooperación. Ayuda a los ciudadanos a entender cómo la UE afecta su vida diaria y los anima a participar en el futuro de Europa. Los eventos también destacan el papel de la UE en proteger la democracia y trabajar en temas importantes como el clima y la seguridad.

**Próximo paso**  
Después del Día de Europa, las instituciones de la UE continuarán con sus programas educativos y culturales. El Parlamento Europeo realizará un Día de Puertas Abiertas en Estrasburgo el 17 de mayo. Las oficinas locales de la UE en los países miembros y en todo el mundo seguirán organizando eventos para conectar con los ciudadanos. La UE también seguirá enfocándose en políticas que promuevan la unidad, la seguridad y la prosperidad.

**Frase clave**  
El Día de Europa 2026 reúne a las personas en toda Europa para celebrar décadas de cooperación, democracia y valores compartidos a través de eventos y actividades atractivas.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a large, circular mosaic made of interlocking puzzle pieces each painted with the flags of the 27 EU Member States, forming the shape of Europe; the mosaic rests on a warm, softly glowing map background, surrounded by stylized, open doors symbolizing accessibility and unity, with gentle beams of blue and gold light radiating outward representing democracy, cooperation, and shared values.
